text: Neural networks seem complicated, but this series breaks them down step by step.
The goal: understand what neural networks do and how they do it — no more black box.
Part 2 covers backpropagation — how neural networks are actually trained on data.
The example problem: predict if a drug dosage (low/medium/high) will be effective.
A straight line can't fit this data — a neural network fits a squiggle instead.
A neural network can fit a squiggle to even very complicated datasets.
A neural network = nodes + connections with parameter values estimated from data.
Parameters are like slope and intercept in linear regression — estimated by fitting.
The bent/curved lines inside nodes (activation functions) are what let the network create squiggles.
Common activation functions: Soft Plus (smooth curve), ReLU (bent line), Sigmoid (S-curve).
Activation function terminology alert: the curved/bent lines inside nodes are called activation functions.
In practice, ReLU and Soft Plus are more common than Sigmoid, despite what tutorials teach.
Hidden layers are the node layers between input and output — you decide how many to use.
Even complex neural networks are built from the same simple parts as this one hidden-layer example.
Step 1: input × weight + bias → x-axis value fed into the activation function.
The activation function's output becomes the y-axis value — here: log(1 + e^2.14) = 2.25.
Only a narrow portion (red box) of the activation function is used — determined by the input range.
Scaling the blue curve by –1.3 flips and stretches it into a new shape.
The second hidden node uses different weights (–2.52, +1.29) and produces the orange curve.
Blue curve + orange curve = green squiggle that fits the dosage data perfectly.
Final step: subtract a bias (0.58) to shift the squiggle down to fit the data.
Prediction: dosage 0.5 → squiggle y = 1.03 → closer to 1 → effective.
Why 'neural network'? Nodes were thought to resemble neurons; connections resemble synapses (1940s–50s).
Weights multiply, biases add — the two types of parameters in any neural network.
With more hidden layers and nodes, a neural network can fit nearly any dataset — that's deep learning.
